Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.

If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 63 actions; a strong score needs about 39 or fewer. You are 1.6x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 31 actions returned the board to a state already seen 31 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 31 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

The timer bar is now showing green on the right side (2 pixels). The game is still going. I've been wasting 63 actions. Let me think about what the goal could be. Looking at the board:
- Blue bar (b) at rows 16-19, cols 12-31 (b) at rows 16-19, cols 12-31
- Two yellow U-shapes (Y) at bottom
- Charcoal (c) and magenta (M) blocks on the right side
- Gray bar (w) at bottom
I notice the blue bar is at row 16, not row 12 as I thought earlier. Let me look at the segmentation again. The blue bar is at rows 16-19. The blue bar moved down! It was at rows 12-15 before, now it's at rows 16-19. Wait, that's because the timer bar changed. Let me check if the blue bar moved or if it's just a different rendering.
Actually, looking at the segmentation output: node 5 (b) has boundary [[16, 12], [16, 31]] which means rows 16-19, cols 12-31. But previously it was at rows 12-15. So the blue bar moved down 4 rows. That's strange. Unless the board shifted.
Wait, let me re-read. Previously node 5 was at [[12, 12], [12, 31]] meaning rows 12-15. Now it's at [[16, 12], [16, 31]] meaning rows 16-19. So the blue bar moved down 4 rows.
This could mean the board is scrolling or the objects are falling. Let me check if the yellow shapes also moved. Previously they were at [[52, 16], [52, 19]] and [[52, 40], [52, 43]]. Now they're still at [[52, 16], [52, 19]] and [[52, 40], [52, 43]]. So the yellow shapes didn't move.
Hmm, the blue bar moved down 4 rows but the yellow shapes didn't. That's odd. Let me check if the board dimensions changed.

Wait, the magenta block is 4 rows tall (rows 4-7), but it's 16 pixels. Let me check: 4 rows x 4 cols = 16 pixels. So it's a 4x4 block at rows 4-7, cols 36-39.
The charcoal block is 3 rows x 4 cols = 12 pixels. So it's a 3x4 block at rows 1-3, cols 36-39.
The magenta block is sitting on top of the charcoal block.
The blue bar is 4 rows x 20 cols = 80 pixels. So it's a 4x20 bar at rows 16-19, cols 12-31.
The yellow shapes are 80 pixels each. They look like U-shapes.
The gray bar is 4 rows x 64 cols = 256 pixels. So it's a 4x64 bar at rows 60-63.
Now I see the board more clearly. The magenta block is sitting on top of the charcoal block. The blue bar is in the middle. The yellow shapes are at the bottom. The gray bar is at the very bottom.
I need to figure out what the goal is. Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is to make the magenta block fall off the charcoal block. Or maybe I need to click on the magenta block to make it fall. Let me try clicking on the magenta block.
